channel.title: Production on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Production on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Creating Playtest Plans
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about creating play test plans.

This is something that’s really important if you’re making an RPG — And yes, this is going to be about be about making them for RPGs, because that’s what I make. I’ve been asked a lot about what procedure I follow And it’s been a little different for some of them and I’ll go over that. I spent a lot of time trying to find the plans. I know I wrote specific plans down. I remember writing the Temple of Elemental Evil play test plan, but I went through all my Troka note archive and I couldn’t find anything. Although I did find an email referencing the vampire play test plan, but it wasn’t attached to the email. So I don’t know, maybe someone else mailed them that and I was just talking with them about it. But in general, I make play test plans when the QA is going to be done out of house. Fallout was in-house and I met with that QA lead.

item.title: A Typical Day Of Making Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a typical day of making Fallout, kind of to give you an insight into what it was like.

And I’m going to do it in the form of a musical. A typical day of making fall. No, we’re not going to do that Scratch that, I’m just going to talk about it. This time I’m going to talk about, to me, a typical day for making Fallout. It’s going to be about a year or so into development.

item.title: Adding A Feature: Then And Now
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about adding a feature to a game back in the day compared to today.

I almost called this why game development costs more, but I think that will attract a lot of angry attention. So what I want to talk about is development costs, and I want to talk about it in the context of why adding a particular feature is harder to do today than it was 20, 30 years ago. I see a lot of people talking about game development and how it takes longer and it costs more. But they often get the underlying reasons wrong, Um, and sometimes I think they deliberately get it wrong, Um, because they’re trying to promote an agenda or get clicks or views, and sometimes they just feel like insulting developers. So it’s like we’re talking about how much it costs and that’ll get people riled up and then they can be mad at developers.

item.title: Why I Don’t Solo Dev
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about why I don’t solo dev.

I’ve been asked this. I’ve been asked: hey, now that you’re semi-retired and you’re only working part-time, why don’t you make games all by yourself? Why don’t you solo dev?

You could do anything you want. Why not do that? So I decided to talk about why I don’t do it. Um, and I’m going to tell you, because there’s actually a bunch of reasons.

item.title: Game Developer Stage Limitations
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game developer stage limitations, which is a somewhat complicated talk.

And yes, it’s dark because in Seattle, once summer ends, it starts getting darker earlier in the evening and it stays dark later in the morning And I get up around. You know, I’m up at this desk by 600..

Sun’s not coming up for another half hour. I’m not waiting for it. This topic on the limitations by game production stage is complicated. It wasn’t something I would have talked about a year or two ago because I had to lay a whole foundation. So I’m going to reference videos while I talk, Recommend you watch the ones I referenced, or at least have watched them, because I’m not going to redefine terms and get lost in talking about game development roles. But first I do want to talk about what I’m not talking about here, because I’ve had. I’ve talked about limitations before. I’ve talked about my own limitations. Um, I have.

item.title: Industry Pendulum Swings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about industry pendulum swings.

Now, what do I mean by that? I’m not talking about Trends. I’m not talking about people going, hey, action RPGs are so hot right now. But I’m also not talking about repeated predictions, like I’m told like every five to seven years that PC gaming is dying again. And it’s not.

What I’m talking about is, over the last four and a half decades I’ve watched things come into the industry and go out of the industry and then come back into the industry and it’s odd to watch these things. And it’s not just game features, it’s Production Way the production’s done, they the way the whole industry is run. So I’m going to talk about some of those, because I kind of want to put some stuff in context so you guys can see that whenever something starts happening in the industry, you have to realize it’s happened before and it’s going to happen again, because these things just go in. And out and in and out, so and out and in and out and in and out. So let me start with one that I first noticed.

item.title: "Eating Your Own Dog Food"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about eating your own dog food.

What do I mean by this? I did a video a while back on how much developers should play games. A lot of people thought I was talking about playing their own games, but I was talking about just playing games in general and one commenter said: no, developers should eat their own dog food, and this is a phrase I’ve heard for decades. I work with a producer who’s been saying it for forever and what it means is if you make a game, you should be playing that game. Often they mean before it ever ships. But I also think that people should play their own games after they ship, because you would be surprised at what remained unpolished or unfinished or just wrong, like a bad design or bad implementation. I’ve mentioned here that I have not won but two games that shipped an earlier build by their publisher, like I was literally holding a CD with a more recent build. That fixed bugs, in some case egregious bugs, in some case bugs that showed up in the very opening, cinematic. But publisher like, no, I’m shipping the earlier version because we got to get this thing out the door.

item.title: Does More Dev Time Mean A Better Game?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about does more Dev time mean a better game?

This is a question from T4 nu1, who asked: hey, Tim, there’s a been a myth going around for ages that more development time equals a better product. Is that myth true? Also, what can’t be fixed with more development time? Ah yes, this ancient myth about more development time. I believe it’s from Mesopotamia 6 or 8 thousand years ago.

item.title: Onboarding New Team Members
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about onboarding new team members.

This is from a question from pooa 8730 who asked looking at Team growth. In those photos they were talking about a video I posted of uh development during Fallout. I’ll put — I’ll link it below: how difficult was it to onboard new people? You need to get new people up to speed as fast as possible and they clearly have no knowledge of existing implemented things, especially in Game Dev where deadlines are super tight. Interesting question. This is another one of those questions where I never would have thought to do a video on this until someone asked, because getting people up to speed is just part of what you always did and so, like a fish surrounded by water, it didn’t occur to me that I was wet. But I do want to say this — and this isn’t uh directed to the person who made the comment, but just something I’ve noticed is onboarding. When I hear that term, it always makes me smile a little bit because it’s one of those terms that have snuck into game development from other places and it makes me realize how much influence is coming in from these places. We never used to use onboarding as a term. In fact, I don’t even think I heard that term before 10 years ago, and I certainly didn’t hear it frequently until about 5 years ago — and suddenly everything’s about or onboarding.

item.title: When Your Game Doesn’t Sell
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about what you should do when your game doesn’t sell.

All been there? Well, I’ve been there. I did a whole video, in fact, on data about my games, talking about their sales figures and how it doesn’t always seem to correlate with critical reviews or team size or budget or any of that. The video is linked Below in the description: — description.

item.title: The Cost Of Moddability
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the cost of modability, specifically the cost of making your video game modable by end users.

And this is a really good topic and it came from a really good question by pelicano 1987 who asked: how much in time money does it cost to make a game modable? Usually, players just think that releasing the dev tools is enough, but it isn’t. When is it viable to think of allowing easy to mod even something like custom maps or Sprites, 3D models, rules and mechanics? How much does a game lose when it changes Focus to allow for player generated content? I’m going to talk about this. I can’t go into specific cost because it varies.

item.title: Stress Testing Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about stress testing games.

This is another question from thequiz who, when giving me a few topic suggestions for upcoming videos, recommended stress testing games. Now let me tell you what that is. Um, this is about. It’s mostly about testing games against mspec machines, but a little more than that. It is not QA.

I’ve talked about quality assurance testing. Uh, I have a couple videos I’ll link below about how we did QA for The Outer Worlds, but this is about. When I talk about stress testing a game, it’s about making sure they work with a wide variety of machines Beyond just mspec, but frequently it is mspec, so the two are related. I do have a video about mpec as well. Um, it’s linked below: stress testing games. You have to think of all the variety of ways that you could make a game slow. You could make a game unreliable and, yes, even crashy.

item.title: Rising Game Budgets
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Rising Game budgets.

This was a question from Irvin Chadwick 5046, who asked: would you be able to give a breakdown on why video games are so much more expensive to make now as compared to 10 years ago? First off, I can compare it to 10 years ago- 20, 30, 40 — and I will — um, I’m also going to assume that this question has nothing to do with sales price. Just to head that off. That’s a completely different discussion of why video games cost more now, or even if they do cost more now, adjusted for inflation, but that’s a whole different discussion. Going to not going to talk about sales price here, I’m going to talk about budget also. This is a general discussion. Notice all the caveats, because I’m just heading off comments.

item.title: Adding New Features After Launch
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about adding new features after launch, and by that I mean I’m going to talk about why it can be very difficult sometimes to add a new feature after the game has shipped.

And I know you’ve all had this experience where you get a new game and you’re playing it and you’re having a lot of fun, but within an hour you’re like I can’t believe this happens, I can’t believe I can’t do this, or I can’t believe this interface is like this, and you wonder why they just don’t change it. Like, in addition to all the bugs they may be fixing in the next patch, why don’t they just fix this one? Why don’t they just add this feature now?

item.title: Communication Between Roles
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about communication between roles, specifically, how to handle all the different roles of people on your development team and getting them to talk to each other.

This is actually a question from Boku, who asks, I wonder, about the different communication channels between roles, which roles are often together and such because they are closely related into velopment — you hinted at level designers and narratives being together, for example. Boku, this is a really good question. It’s also a really serious question because it has to do with how you’re going to manage a team, especially as teams get bigger. This gets more and more important — and get them to work together. Even though there are several of them, many of different types of people. Some people want to have these discussions, some people will dominate the discussions, other people don’t want to have discussions at all, but you need to get people to communicate, and at the high level. I would just answer and say this: communic, crossr communication.

item.title: Design Fallbacks
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about design fallbacks now.

I’ve talked about these before and in fact I’m answering a question from gkid 100 who asked: can you make a video about fallbacks? What is the process for designing a less resource heavy version of a system or a replacement for a system? I think I talked about this. I know I did design fallbacks heavily during The Outer Worlds, but I had them in previous games, just not so structured like. There literally was a section in the design document for the The Outer Worlds. It’s called design fallbacks and, by the way, when I talk about design document there are some people out there that still think I’m talking about I have a paper document or a word dock. The Outer Worlds one was spread across multiple, uh, web pages. I, we used Confluence that were constantly Ed, Ed and interl, so it was like a living document. So you know, if you went to a page you could not only see the design. But you could see people’s comments on it. You could see who was assigned to it. If you clicked on that, you could go through even the schedules and see idea of when this thing would be made.

item.title: Min Specs And Ratings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game minimum specifications and ratings.

This comes from a question from suin, who asks: how do games get their system requirements? Since Tim’s a programmer, I’d imagine understanding the system is pretty important. But is determining the requirements part of the programmer’s job, or QA, or marketing, or the producer? How much time you spent figuring that out? And is a game designed for a spec early on? For example, our game has to run on 480p and 128 Mega of Ram, or at the end, when the project is finalized, when everything that’s going in is in and the minimum is then gauged.

item.title: Localization Questions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about localization.

Localization is when you make a version of your game for outside, for distribution outside of whatever country you originally made your game for. Now, this is an answer to a question from Johnny Katswell, who asked: you mentioned adaptation. You mentioned adapting your games to different cultures and languages. I didn’t find a video on this topic on your channel, so let me ask my questions here. By the way, I really thought I had a video on this and I didn’t so good catch Johnny. Johnny had four questions.

item.title: Flow
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about flow.

I’m not talking about Flo, The Sassy kiss my grits waitress from the TV sitcom Alice. I’m talking about Flo as in the The Zone, The Mind state that you get into that allows this really efficient, uninterrupted work output. If you know what flow is, you just know what it is. If you never Flow, then you probably are confused. Right now it’s hard to Define flow. Um, it takes a while to get into it.

item.title: Creativity Under Pressure
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about creativity Under Pressure.

Now, what made me think of this, of doing This relates to a bunch of videos I’ve done before and I’m going to mention them as I go along. But I read a quote from Carl BOS, who was a band member of craftwork, and he said — quote: creativity sometimes starts with a deadline and with a lack of money and with only a few possibilities, with not too many options. Wow I that’s a good expression of doing art in a medium where just making that art costs money and making that art requires a group and making that art is part of an industry.

item.title: The Outer Worlds Vertical Slice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I have a very special video to show you.

It is the The Outer Worlds’s vertical slice. Before I get in, let me give you some context, and actually, before I give you context, let me thank obsidian and especially Mikey Dowling for arranging this and letting me show this to you. I want to show you the vertical slice for many reasons, and let me let me explain what a vertical slice is first, if you watched my production stages video, which I link below, I talk about the different stages that a game goes through when it’s being developed. The vertical slice stage is when you make a level of the game or a map or, in this case, an outdoor map with a few connecting Maps. I think there was a town map and three dungeon Maps.

item.title: Arcanum Data
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum data.

So the last few weeks — well, since I’ve I I posted this almost a year ago, I think — since I converted Arcanum to be a Windows, a modern Windows app, it’s way easier to debug it I can put break points anywhere. I can run the game in a window, you know, hit the break points wherever I want, look at what is going on, because you know it’s been 25 years more since code started on that game. I don’t remember how everything works and I didn’t write everything. There were three other programmers, so I’ve been going through it for various reasons, and one of them I was doing recently was I was trying to see how the data flow was handled, basically what was loaded when in the game. And first of all, let me just say there is a lot of data in this game. I look at it and I have to remind myself that the entire game was made by essentially 14 people. Yes, there’s a lot more people in the credits, but you know I’m not counting people who did localization and production and you know, uh made the boxes and handled advertisements and things like that. I’m just talking about the actual development team. It was 14 people.

item.title: Time Estimation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about time estimation.

I’ve had received a lot of requests over the last year for people asking for a good method to estimate tasks, because this is a frequent contentious point between people and either the people they manage or their managers, where time estimates are just off and then upset situations happen. I’m going to talk about what I consider to be a good way of making estimates and problems of over and underestimating. Now a lot of people have asked. The most recent request was from aen Targaryen 1390, who asked: can you please do a video on time estimate, estimations and planning and how to handle situations where the boss says it needs to be done in X days and you say it will take 3x days, okay, one reason I put off answering this question for a long time is it’s very, very complicated.

item.title: The Horizontal Slice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the horizontal slice.

I will explain what I mean by that. I’m actually going to answer a question from John flyer 4873, who asks: do you have a particular balance of quest content that you try to reach? Is there a process that goes into determining how long SL complex the main quest is versus how many side quests there are and where they are in your world? Let me just say that I didn’t used to have any process for that, it was just feel it as you go, and you can kind of see that in Fallout and Arcanum, where there are big chunks of the world that don’t have anything in them, um, there are, there are clusters of places where there are a lot more quests than other places. Um, Temple, of course, was made um off a module and, even though we had trouble with some areas were too dense.

item.title: The Missing R&D Department
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the missing R&D department, and by R&D I mean research and development.

This is a department that I think is missing at most, if not all, game companies, and it’s super important. This is the skunkworks group that really good ideas come out of um. If you’ve watched my Fallout videos, it’s, I think, how Fallout was created: accidentally, because the initial group was an impromptu, random Skunk Works that was thrown together on their own time outside of the normal channel. So by the time production really started, we had a really good idea of what we wanted to make.

item.title: Common Sources Of Programming Frustration
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about common sources of programming frustration.

This is probably going to be a long video. It’s also me talking to programmers, so while it may be of interest to non-game development programmers, it’s probably only academically interesting to any of the game development people who aren’t programmers, although I there are some things in here I think some production people should look at, or, I’m sorry, just be aware of, um, because I got a couple production things to say at the end of the video. But let me also say that this is not going to be the same video as the one I did on challenges facing programmers in game development. That’s more of a team related thing about you know, the challenges of working with non-programmers. This is more of things that will happen when you’re programming. That will frustrate you, aggravate you, throw you into a rage and pretty much sometimes put a roadblock in on development.

item.title: Fallout Development Timeline
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do the Fallout development timeline.

I’ve been putting together notes — I’ve mentioned this before about the making a Fallout and I think I’ve created a pretty definitive timeline here. So it’s kind of long. It’s probably going to be kind of long. There are a few pictures, but not a lot.

So if you need to go get coffee or you need to go to the bathroom, do it now. Don’t pause me later. I’ll know if you pause me, but this is what I put together from probably like five years of notes. Um, maybe a little more. Let me start a little pre Fallout, which is important.

item.title: Five Traits In A Good Producer
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about what makes a good producer.

This actually comes from a question from Phil sp26, who asks: I was wondering if you would mind sharing what makes a good producer. What are you looking for and advice for those who plan on heading into that role? That is a very good question, Phil, because I’m always surprised at what people think they wanted a producer, or even that they don’t know what a producer is. The producer is, he’s kind of at the top of the chain. Most people think the game director is, but in some ways the producer is because they’re handling everything that’s going on and making sure it’s getting done and interfacing with the publisher and interfacing with localization, interfacing with external groups that are doing work you may have, you know, maybe you’ve externed some of your art. They’re handling all that stuff, even if they have assistant producers who are directly handling it. They’re managing those people, so producers do a lot of stuff and you may think the answer I would give you is: I like a producer that does what I say. No, producers need to be a little tough because they work with all kinds of different people and they need to. They’re the ones who often put their foot down and say this has to be done or it won’t go into the game. Yeah, the game director does that a lot, but sometimes the producers — the one that even has to tell the game director you’re out of time, you’re out of money, or that person isn’t available and anymore they’re moving on to something else.

item.title: How To Pick The Right Project
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how to pick the right project, and this comes from a question from Bridges, chroma 1832, who asks: can you talk about how project approval decisions are made in game companies?

Do they follow the will of a singular lead designer or do they get talked about a lot before any actual effort is put into it? Does something akin to military democracy existed game companies? Now, I wasn’t 100% sure, uh, what Bridges meant by that, so I asked for clarification. Do you mean deciding what project to make, which led to a yes and a further for smaller companies and Studios? When they have more Choice with their own ideas, how do they pick the right one within the company? What project do they make? So let me talk about that, because I’ve done that several times where I’ve been at the beginning of a project and having to decide what project to make, and I think every time it’s boiled down to the same thing and so the high level answer to this, the TLD, DW, is going to be: pick something that leads into your team’s um strengths and the size and time you have available to you. So I mean, if you know your team is going to be a certain size and made up of a certain number of individuals and you only have so much time — and by time I ultimately mean Budget — to make it there, that’s pretty much everything you need to know what kind of game you’re going to make. So let’s dive down into that, so by size slash time, I basically mean this is the point where you select the scope of your game, and by scope I don’t necessarily mean how long it will take to play the game. I mean it basically brows down to how many features you want in the game, the more features you have. The richer the game is, but also it takes a lot longer to make, not only because the individual features take a long time to work on, but there will often be a lot of interactions between those features. Once you put in lots of skills and lots of perks and lots of traits and lots of backgrounds, those are going to interact. And then it will bring up questions about: hey, there’s a bunch of bonuses that are very similar. Do they stack? Hey, these bonuses all work on aspects of this skill.

item.title: Shorter Times And Smaller Budgets
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about what to do when time and budget are tight.

In fact, this is a answer to Abraham drinken 2534, who asks or says: I would like to see you talk about the hierarchy of needs within development and what’s are some of the first things to go when time and budget get tight. Well, before I say that, let me remind you that people often talk about a triangle in development with three sides: good, fast and cheap — and you have to pick two. So if you pick fast and cheap, you’re not going to get good. I don’t know how much I believe in this situation, because first of all, once you go cheap, I don’t see you getting good or fast. I don’t see how you get fast with cheap.

item.title: Game Production Stages
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game production stages.

This is actually an answer to a question I got from Charlie K 5964, who asks, as a follow-up to the ideas video, what should go into the Prototype. Should it be a minimum viable product or should it things like art be included? Now, that’s a really good question, but couple things, um, different companies call different things a prototype. So what I thought I’d do is walk through the stages that my games have gone through and I’ll tell you the names I give them.

item.title: Issues...And How To Fix Them
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I not only want to talk about issues that come up in game development, but how to fix them.

I know it’s dark, but Uncle Tim’s got a lot of work to do today so I had to film this. Early Dawn is still about oh, 20 minutes away.

So I’ve talked about a lot of issues on this channel. I’ve some that generated a great deal of comments and conflicting comments, things about work, life balance and crunch stuff about team size, project length, the video I made on game developer caution. So before I start talking about how I think we should talk about solving some of these, let’s just talk about those videos and kind of a context around them. First of all, if you go back and look at the vide and listen carefully, I’m not blaming any group for those causes, for those problems. I’m careful to avoid that. I’m careful to say these are the things that happened, but I don’t say why and I don’t say whose fault it is.

item.title: Game Development Time
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game development time.

I’ve gotten a lot of questions from a lot of people. This is maybe the most commonly asked question, at least in the last few weeks, because I’ve gotten at least I don’t know four or five of these. Uh geomancer HT asks: please talk about the.

It’s ready when it’s done and a delayed game is eventually good. Tetramir 1 asks: uh, talking about a length of development would be interesting subject, because teams have gotten bigger but games take longer too. Yeti catcher 1780 asked: do you think games coming out under shorter deadlines means only the best things get put in the game, so it’s better, or do you think it’s always better for them to have huge development Cycles and our own bite your own teeth asked? Here’s another one: when to cut, when to polish, what to look out for, when a feature won’t make the cut, how do you get it past the finish line? These are pretty much.

item.title: Perfect Is The Enemy Of Good
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a common phrase I hear in the industry called perfect is the enemy of good.

I think I didn’t hear this until about mid-2000s when I was at carbine. This is a phrase that basically means the time you take to make a feature perfect will take away time from making other features good, and it’s commonly given as a warning that you shouldn’t spend too much time on any one feature if you’re not bringing all the other features up to parity with it. Sometimes I’ve heard this as an excuse to get rid of features early when you can’t make them good enough. Um. I think it’s a good idea.

item.title: Team Size
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a topic that was suggested to me by a co-worker, or I should say by someone who’s working at one of the companies I’m currently Contracting at, uh, and he asked: I’d love to hear your thoughts on the ever expanding team size on projects and the impact that has on the process of making games.

And that’s a good question, because been doing this a long time and teams are steadily getting bigger. They don’t always get bigger. I’ve gone from some bigger teams to some smaller ones, but in general, games are taking more and more people to make, so I thought I’d talk about that.

item.title: Crunch
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about crunch.

Been a lot of people saying a lot of things about crunch. People been saying a lot of things about crunch for decades. Uh, I don’t know how much I can add to this other than my own experience with crunch, in both being on crunch that was imposed on me, being on crutches that I imposed, being on crunches that were caused by other people being on crunches at high cost. So it gives me a pretty big perspective on crunch. And let me just Define crunch first of all. Crunch is the extra work usually at the end of a game, but it could occur before making a demo or a conference. It’s a crunch that happens when the game’s development Gets behind schedule. So you have to work hard — extra hours, weekends — to make up and get the game back on schedule now to me, one of the most annoying things I hear, and I’m going to be very careful how I say this. I hear this a lot. Quote: crunch is always the result of bad management. Unquote.

item.title: My GNW Award
item.description: I forgot to mention in my previous video that Canal was used on so many projects at interplay and saved many months of development time on those projects that I won an award. Get a load of that.

item.title: How To Build A Motivated Team (1996)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I’m going to read to you a presentation that I made — I think it was sometime in 1996 — at interplay, on how to build a motivated team.

I mentioned this in a previous video. I didn’t have the presentation for years, I just had the notes I used to write it. But a few years ago an ex-co-worker from interplay approached me and he had a printed copy of my talk. So I have the actual thing that I presented, and I will. I I wrote a preface to it because this is written 27 years ago. I think I was 30 years old. It was my first and last time being a producer, so I thought this needed a little introduction. So I’ll read you the instruction and then I’ll do the presentation. So here we go, so sometime in late 96, Brian Fargo asked me to address the other producers in our regular producer meeting. He liked how I was running my Fallout project. And he said he was equally impressed with the high morale of the team. He asked me to talk about how I managed to do that when many of the other producers were complaining to him of low team morale and low work ethic. Bear in mind when reading this: I don’t consider myself to be a particularly good producer then or now, but the bar was low for good production and interplay, not because they, The Producers, weren’t trying, but because we had absolutely no training. In fact, when I asked the executive producer there for training, he said that’s not how they did things. They just threw people in the pool to see who could swim well. I didn’t think being held up as an example of a great producer was a good idea for me, but it was something I didn’t understand at the time and the document below will.

item.title: Playtesting The Outer Worlds
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about play testing in the inner worlds.

The reason I want to focus on that is on some previous videos I talked about examples of bad play testing. That happened on my game, specifically Temple of Elemental Evil, where the external QA group decided they didn’t like playing bards, so they never played them, and we shipped with a really bad crash bug. Even though I played with a all-bard group, I never saw the bug, but they never played bards, so I was mad.

item.title: The Trials of The Temple of Elemental Evil
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim, so by now we’ve I’ve talked about troika’s games, Arcanum and bloodlines.

I’ve even talked about the secret DOD project, and I’ve talked about our demos for Lord of the Rings and for our post-apocalyptic game, Epic. I’ve not talked about Temple, so let’s talk about Temple. Temple it’s um troika’s worst scoring reviewed game. I think it’s the worst reviewed game of my career. I kind of want to explain how it became that and, for people who think I’m just gonna give excuses, this is an explanation and just so you know, everything I’m going to talk about is either the result of a bad decision I made — or because of a contract that I read, agreed to and signed, so I’m to blame for all this is. Get that out of the way, and now let me talk about what actually happened when we were making it, so towards the end, actually after Arcanum. So after Arcanum, we started working on Journey. To the Center of Arcanum and then that didn’t happen and so we started doing Lord of the Rings and that didn’t happen.
